title: Economic Stress model mediation example
text: Also install sandwich package robust standard errors, don’t load . Summarise dataset: goal fit mediation model Figure 4.2 2017 Introduction Process text Andrew Hayes. model, estress, ese, sex tenure predict affect; five variables predict withdraw. already scale average scores estress, ese, affect, withdraw. also gather construct reliabilities table 1 paper: using regression calibration, need think regression model, one --time.
code: library(rccme) library(modelsummary) # for model summary summary(estress) #>      tenure          estress         affect         withdraw     #>  Min.   : 0.000   Min.   :1.00   Min.   :1.000   Min.   :1.000   #>  1st Qu.: 0.940   1st Qu.:3.50   1st Qu.:1.160   1st Qu.:1.000   #>  Median : 4.000   Median :4.50   Median :1.330   Median :2.000   #>  Mean   : 5.929   Mean   :4.62   Mean   :1.598   Mean   :2.321   #>  3rd Qu.: 8.980   3rd Qu.:5.50   3rd Qu.:1.830   3rd Qu.:3.000   #>  Max.   :33.000   Max.   :7.00   Max.   :5.000   Max.   :7.000   #>       sex              age             ese        #>  Min.   :0.0000   Min.   :23.00   Min.   :2.530   #>  1st Qu.:0.0000   1st Qu.:36.00   1st Qu.:5.000   #>  Median :1.0000   Median :44.00   Median :5.730   #>  Mean   :0.6183   Mean   :43.79   Mean   :5.607   #>  3rd Qu.:1.0000   3rd Qu.:51.00   3rd Qu.:6.330   #>  Max.   :1.0000   Max.   :71.00   Max.   :7.000 cons_rel <- list(   "estress" = .72, "affect" = .88, "withdraw" = .73, "ese" = .95 )

title: Economic Stress model mediation example
text: model, estress ese scale predictors, .e., measured error. sex tenure assumed measured without error. Prior regression, calibrate estress ese scores. pass covariates measured without error using z_mat argument. Now can run regression: also run regression original variables: see estress coefficient larger calibrated variable.
code: cal_for_aff_out <- rccme_calib_me(   estress[, c("estress", "ese")],   rel_vec = c(cons_rel$estress, cons_rel$ese),   z_mat = estress[, c("sex", "tenure")] ) head(cal_for_aff_out) #>       estress      ese #> [1,] 5.643158 5.340113 #> [2,] 4.764247 6.023981 #> [3,] 5.290965 5.276879 #> [4,] 3.594975 4.427072 #> [5,] 4.629474 4.901408 #> [6,] 5.690184 5.071872 estress$estress_to_aff <- cal_for_aff_out[, "estress"] estress$ese_to_aff <- cal_for_aff_out[, "ese"] (fit_aff_cal <- lm(   affect ~ estress_to_aff + ese_to_aff + sex + tenure, estress )) #>  #> Call: #> lm(formula = affect ~ estress_to_aff + ese_to_aff + sex + tenure,  #>     data = estress) #>  #> Coefficients: #>    (Intercept)  estress_to_aff      ese_to_aff             sex          tenure   #>        1.45953         0.22453        -0.14667        -0.01102        -0.01174 (fit_aff_no_cal <- lm(   affect ~ estress + ese + sex + tenure, estress )) #>  #> Call: #> lm(formula = affect ~ estress + ese + sex + tenure, data = estress) #>  #> Coefficients: #> (Intercept)      estress          ese          sex       tenure   #>     1.78549      0.15934     -0.15488      0.01479     -0.01084

title: Economic Stress model mediation example
text: model, estress, ese affect scale predictors, .e., measured error. sex tenure assumed measured without error. Prior regression, calibrate estress, ese affect scores: Now can run regression: also run regression original variables: see estress affect coefficients larger calibrated variables.
code: cal_for_wth_out <- rccme_calib_me(   estress[, c("estress", "ese", "affect")],   rel_vec = c(cons_rel$estress, cons_rel$ese, cons_rel$affect),   z_mat = estress[, c("sex", "tenure")] ) head(cal_for_wth_out) #>       estress      ese   affect #> [1,] 5.784934 5.329278 2.501453 #> [2,] 4.632448 6.034053 1.091612 #> [3,] 5.403243 5.268299 2.321956 #> [4,] 3.507735 4.433739 1.220640 #> [5,] 4.482927 4.912607 1.101863 #> [6,] 5.612494 5.077809 1.554001 estress$estress_to_wth <- cal_for_wth_out[, "estress"] estress$ese_to_wth <- cal_for_wth_out[, "ese"] estress$affect_to_wth <- cal_for_wth_out[, "affect"] (fit_wth_cal <- lm(   withdraw ~ estress_to_wth + ese_to_wth + affect_to_wth + sex + tenure,   estress )) #>  #> Call: #> lm(formula = withdraw ~ estress_to_wth + ese_to_wth + affect_to_wth +  #>     sex + tenure, data = estress) #>  #> Coefficients: #>    (Intercept)  estress_to_wth      ese_to_wth   affect_to_wth             sex   #>      2.8152882      -0.1691593      -0.2108133       0.8623629       0.1461277   #>         tenure   #>      0.0001724 (fit_wth_no_cal <- lm(   withdraw ~ estress + ese + affect + sex + tenure, estress )) #>  #> Call: #> lm(formula = withdraw ~ estress + ese + affect + sex + tenure,  #>     data = estress) #>  #> Coefficients: #> (Intercept)      estress          ese       affect          sex       tenure   #>    2.746089    -0.093542    -0.212106     0.707137     0.127391    -0.002065

title: Economic Stress model mediation example
code: modelsummary(   list(     "Affect (No Calibration)" = fit_aff_no_cal,     "Affect (With Calibration)" = fit_aff_cal,     "Withdraw (No Calibration)" = fit_wth_no_cal,     "Withdraw (With Calibration)" = fit_wth_cal   ),   estimate = "{estimate} ({std.error})", statistic = "{p.value}",   # Set standard error to HCSEs and omit distracting fit indices   gof_omit = "IC|F|Log", vcov = "HC4",   # Rename calibrated variables:   coef_rename = c(     "estress_to_aff" = "estress", "estress_to_wth" = "estress",     "ese_to_aff" = "ese", "ese_to_wth" = "ese", "affect_to_wth" = "affect"   ) )

title: Economic Stress model mediation example
text: Pollack, J. M., VanEpps, E. M., & Hayes, . F. (2012). moderating role social ties entrepreneurs’ depressed affect withdrawal intentions response economic stress. Journal Organizational Behavior, 33(6), 789–810. https://doi.org/10.1002/JOB.1794

title: Protest: A moderation example
text: Also install sandwich package robust standard errors, don’t load . Summarise dataset: goal fit moderation model Figure 10.2 2017 Introduction Process text Andrew Hayes. model, sexism interacts levels protest predict liking. already scale average scores sexism liking. also gather construct reliabilities table 1 paper:
code: library(rccme) library(modelsummary) # for model summary summary(protest) #>      subnum         protest          sexism          angry       #>  Min.   :  7.0   Min.   :0.000   Min.   :2.870   Min.   :1.000   #>  1st Qu.: 68.0   1st Qu.:0.000   1st Qu.:4.500   1st Qu.:1.000   #>  Median :115.0   Median :1.000   Median :5.120   Median :1.000   #>  Mean   :121.8   Mean   :1.031   Mean   :5.117   Mean   :2.124   #>  3rd Qu.:182.0   3rd Qu.:2.000   3rd Qu.:5.620   3rd Qu.:2.000   #>  Max.   :240.0   Max.   :2.000   Max.   :7.000   Max.   :7.000   #>      liking         respappr     #>  Min.   :1.000   Min.   :1.500   #>  1st Qu.:5.000   1st Qu.:4.000   #>  Median :5.830   Median :5.250   #>  Mean   :5.637   Mean   :4.866   #>  3rd Qu.:6.500   3rd Qu.:5.750   #>  Max.   :7.000   Max.   :7.000 cons_rel <- list(   "sexism" = .75, "liking" = .88 )

title: Protest: A moderation example
text: calibrate sexism score since ’s predictor. pass dummy variables two levels protest since predictors need calibrating.
code: head(cov_lik <- model.matrix(~ factor(protest), protest)) #>   (Intercept) factor(protest)1 factor(protest)2 #> 1           1                0                1 #> 2           1                0                0 #> 3           1                0                1 #> 4           1                0                1 #> 5           1                0                1 #> 6           1                1                0 cal_scores_for_lik_out <- rccme_calib_me(   protest[, c("sexism")],   rel_vec = cons_rel$sexism,   z_mat = cov_lik[, -1] # remove intercept ) head(cal_scores_for_lik_out) #>       [,1] #> 1 4.912301 #> 2 4.458092 #> 3 5.009350 #> 4 5.382615 #> 5 5.472199 #> 6 5.621909 protest$sexism_to_lik <- cal_scores_for_lik_out[, 1]

title: Protest: A moderation example
text: coefficients different.
code: (fit_cal <- lm(   liking ~ sexism_to_lik * factor(protest),   protest )) #>  #> Call: #> lm(formula = liking ~ sexism_to_lik * factor(protest), data = protest) #>  #> Coefficients: #>                    (Intercept)                   sexism_to_lik   #>                         8.5197                         -0.6329   #>               factor(protest)1                factor(protest)2   #>                        -5.7057                         -4.8265   #> sexism_to_lik:factor(protest)1  sexism_to_lik:factor(protest)2   #>                         1.2072                          1.0419 (fit_no_cal <- lm(   liking ~ sexism * factor(protest),   protest )) #>  #> Call: #> lm(formula = liking ~ sexism * factor(protest), data = protest) #>  #> Coefficients: #>             (Intercept)                   sexism         factor(protest)1   #>                  7.7062                  -0.4725                  -4.1288   #>        factor(protest)2  sexism:factor(protest)1  sexism:factor(protest)2   #>                 -3.4908                   0.9012                   0.7778 modelsummary(   list(     "Without Calibration" = fit_no_cal,     "With Calibration" = fit_cal   ),   estimate = "{estimate} ({std.error})", statistic = "{p.value}",   # Set standard error to HCSEs and omit distracting fit indices   gof_omit = "IC|F|Log", vcov = "HC4",   # Rename sexism_to_lik:   coef_rename = c("sexism_to_lik" = "sexism") )

title: Protest: A moderation example
text: Garcia, D. M., Schmitt, M. T., Branscombe, N. R., & Ellemers, N. (2010). Women’s reactions ingroup members protest discriminatory treatment: importance beliefs inequality response appropriateness. European Journal Social Psychology, 40(5), 733–745. https://doi.org/10.1002/ejsp.644

title: Authors and Citation
text: James Uanhoro. Author, maintainer. Nivedita Bhaktha. Contributor.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Uanhoro J (2026). rccme: Regression Calibration Classical Measurement Error. R package version 0.0.1.9001, https://jamesuanhoro.github.io/rccme/.
code: @Manual{,   title = {rccme: Regression Calibration for Classical Measurement Error},   author = {James Uanhoro},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.0.1.9001},   url = {https://jamesuanhoro.github.io/rccme/}, }

what: Regression Calibration for Classical Measurement Error
title: Regression Calibration for Classical Measurement Error
text: goal rccme produce regression calibrated scores downstream regression models.

title: Regression Calibration for Classical Measurement Error
text: can install latest version rccme :
code: install.packages(   'rccme',   repos = c(     'https://jamesuanhoro.r-universe.dev',     'https://cloud.r-project.org'   ) )

title: Regression Calibration for Classical Measurement Error
text: show approach model :
code: library(rccme)  library(lavaan) # for data source and measurement models ## This is lavaan 0.6-21 ## lavaan is FREE software! Please report any bugs. dat <- HolzingerSwineford1939[, paste0("x", 1:9)]  sem_fit <- sem(   paste(     "F1 =~ x1 + x2 + x3",     "F2 =~ x4 + x5 + x6",     "x9 ~ F1 + F2 + x7 + x8",     "F1 + F2 ~~ x7 + x8",     sep = "\n"   ),   dat,   std.lv = TRUE )  # two-step using factor analysis ---- cfa_f1 <- cfa("F1 =~ x1 + x2 + x3", dat, std.lv = TRUE) cfa_f2 <- cfa("F2 =~ x4 + x5 + x6", dat, std.lv = TRUE) score_f1 <- lavaan::lavPredict(cfa_f1, se = "standard") score_f2 <- lavaan::lavPredict(cfa_f2, se = "standard") se_f1 <- unname(attr(score_f1, "se")[[1]][, 1, drop = TRUE]) se_f2 <- unname(attr(score_f2, "se")[[1]][, 1, drop = TRUE])  # save factor scores dat$f1 <- score_f1 dat$f2 <- score_f2  # calibrate factor scores rc_fs <- rccme_calib_me(   dat[, c("f1", "f2")],   w_se_vec = c(se_f1, se_f2), z_mat = dat[, c("x7", "x8")] )  # save calibrated factor scores dat$f1_rc <- rc_fs[, 1] dat$f2_rc <- rc_fs[, 2]  # two-step using sum-score ---- # compute standardised sum scores dat$std_1 <- scale(rowMeans(dat[, c("x1", "x2", "x3")])) dat$std_2 <- scale(rowMeans(dat[, c("x4", "x5", "x6")])) # compute coefficient alpha rel_1 <- psych::alpha(dat[, c("x1", "x2", "x3")])$total$raw_alpha ## Warning in response.frequencies(x, max = max): response.frequency has been ## deprecated and replaced with responseFrequecy.  Please fix your call  ## Number of categories should be increased  in order to count frequencies. rel_2 <- psych::alpha(dat[, c("x4", "x5", "x6")])$total$raw_alpha ## Warning in response.frequencies(x, max = max): response.frequency has been ## deprecated and replaced with responseFrequecy.  Please fix your call  ## Number of categories should be increased  in order to count frequencies. # calibrate sum scores rc_ss <- rccme_calib_me(   dat[, c("std_1", "std_2")],   # Add `standard = TRUE` for standardised sum scores   rel_vec = c(rel_1, rel_2), z_mat = dat[, c("x7", "x8")], standard = TRUE )  # save calibrated sum scores dat$std_1_rc <- rc_ss[, 1] dat$std_2_rc <- rc_ss[, 2]  # model results ---- # sum-score without measurement error correction fit_ss <- lm(x9 ~ std_1 + std_2 + x7 + x8, dat) # sum-score without measurement error correction fit_ss_rc <- lm(x9 ~ std_1_rc + std_2_rc + x7 + x8, dat) # with standard factor scores fit_fs <- lm(x9 ~ f1 + f2 + x7 + x8, dat) # with calibrated factor scores fit_fs_rc <- lm(x9 ~ f1_rc + f2_rc + x7 + x8, dat)  # SEM results sem_fit |>   parameterestimates() |>   subset(op == "~") ##    lhs op rhs    est    se      z pvalue ci.lower ci.upper ## 7   x9  ~  F1  0.464 0.078  5.945  0.000    0.311    0.616 ## 8   x9  ~  F2 -0.018 0.065 -0.285  0.776   -0.145    0.108 ## 9   x9  ~  x7  0.191 0.046  4.138  0.000    0.101    0.282 ## 10  x9  ~  x8  0.219 0.054  4.061  0.000    0.113    0.324 # Comparing the methods modelsummary::modelsummary(   list(     "Sum score" = fit_ss,     "Factor scores" = fit_fs,     "Calibrated sum scores" = fit_ss_rc,     "Calibrated factor scores" = fit_fs_rc   ),   # Rename calibrated variables:   coef_rename = c(     "std_1" = "F1", "std_2" = "F2",     "std_1_rc" = "F1", "std_2_rc" = "F2",     "f1" = "F1", "f2" = "F2",     "f1_rc" = "F1", "f2_rc" = "F2"   ),   output = "markdown", gof_omit = "IC|R2|Log|F|RMSE|Obs", vcov = "HC4" )

what: Calibrate scores under measurement error — rccme_calib_me
title: Calibrate scores under measurement error — rccme_calib_me
text: Calibrate scores measurement error

title: Calibrate scores under measurement error — rccme_calib_me
code: rccme_calib_me(   w_mat,   rel_vec = NULL,   w_se_vec = NULL,   w_se_mat = NULL,   z_mat = matrix(0, nrow = nrow(w_mat), ncol = 0),   rescale = TRUE,   standard = FALSE )

title: Calibrate scores under measurement error — rccme_calib_me
text: w_mat (matrix) matrix trait estimates n respondents p latent variables rel_vec (vector) vector marginal reliability p latent variables w_se_vec (vector) vector standard errors measurement p latent variables w_se_mat (matrix) matrix trait estimates standard-errors n respondents p latent variables z_mat (matrix) matrix error-free covariates n respondents q covariates rescale (logical) trait estimates standard errors re-scaled? Default TRUE. variables rescaled assumption trait estimates conditioned background variables marginal variance latent trait 1. true standardised latent variables CFA default prior variance assumption 1 IRT score estimates. rescaling ensures trait coefficients correct standardised traits. standard (logical) relevant passing reliability. TRUE, attempt return standardised version calibrated scores FALSE (default), attempt standardisation.

title: Calibrate scores under measurement error — rccme_calib_me
text: Calibrated trait estimates.

title: rccme (development version)
text: Added tests Added vignettes

title: rccme 0.0.1
text: EAP scores IRT models heteroskedastic measurement error, regression factor scores CFA models homoskedastic heteroskedastic error, sum scores reliability estimate. Handles missing data error-free covariates using FIML estimation
